title: Ragnarök: The End and Rebirth
summary: The seeress sat in the shadows, her eyes white as milk, her voice rising from a place deeper than mortal memory.
full_text:
The seeress sat in the shadows, her eyes white as milk, her voice rising from a place deeper than mortal memory. She spoke of what was to come—Ragnarök, the doom of the gods, the twilight that would swallow even the brightest halls of Asgard.

It begins, she said, with the death of Baldr the Beautiful. When the purest of the gods falls to Loki's trickery and a mistletoe dart, the world begins its long slide toward destruction. Then comes Fimbulwinter—three winters without a summer, snow falling from every direction, the sun giving no warmth. Brothers turn against brothers, fathers against sons, and the bonds of kinship shatter like ice. The world descends into chaos even before the monsters arrive.

The wolves Sköll and Hati, who have chased the sun and moon across the sky since time began, finally catch their prey. The sun goes dark. The moon vanishes. The stars fall from the heavens like dying embers. The earth shakes so violently that mountains topple and trees are ripped from the soil.

Then the chains break. Fenrir the wolf, bound by the gods since his youth, snaps his fetters with a howl that cracks the sky. Jörmungandr, the Midgard Serpent, rises from the ocean depths, flooding the lands with salt water and venomous mist. Loki breaks free from his underground prison and steers Naglfar, the ship made from dead men's nails, toward the battlefield of Vígríðr. The fire giants of Muspelheim, led by Surtr with his sword brighter than the sun, march across the rainbow bridge Bifröst and shatter it behind them.

Heimdallr, the watchman of the gods, raises Gjallarhorn to his lips. The ancient horn sounds across all nine worlds, its call piercing and terrible. Odin rides to Mímir's well for one last counsel. Yggdrasil, the world tree, groans and trembles. Every creature in every realm knows that the end has come.

The gods arm themselves. Odin, in his golden helmet, leads the Einherjar—the warriors who have feasted and trained in Valhalla for this very day. Thor swings Mjölnir, his hammer crackling with lightning. Freyr, though he has given away his magic sword for love, fights with antlers. They stand on the field of Vígríðr, one hundred leagues in each direction, facing the assembled forces of chaos.

The battle is cataclysmic. Odin charges at Fenrir, but the wolf swallows him whole. Vidarr, Odin's son, avenges his father by forcing Fenrir's jaws apart and tearing him asunder. Thor engages Jörmungandr and crushes the serpent's head with his hammer, but takes nine steps before the serpent's venom kills him. Freyr falls to Surtr's flaming blade. Týr and Garmr, the hound of Hel, kill each other. Loki and Heimdallr exchange mortal wounds. Surtr raises his sword and hurls fire across the world, burning everything—the fields, the forests, the mountains, the seas. The earth itself sinks beneath the waves.

But the seeress did not stop there. From the sea, a new world rises—green and fertile and beautiful. The surviving gods gather on the fields of Iðavöllr: Vidarr and Váli, Magni and Móði, and Baldr and Höðr, reconciled and returned from the dead. Two humans, Líf and Lífþrasir, emerge from hiding within the wood Hoddmímis holt, where they have survived on morning dew. From them, a new humanity will spring.

Endings are beginnings. From destruction comes renewal. Hope survives all.
moral: From destruction comes renewal; hope survives all.

title: Robin Hood
summary: In the days when King Richard the Lionheart was away fighting in the Crusades and his brother Prince John ruled England with greed and cruelty, a young man from Locksley in Nottinghamshire was forced into the greenwood.
full_text:
In the days when King Richard the Lionheart was away fighting in the Crusades and his brother Prince John ruled England with greed and cruelty, a young man from Locksley in Nottinghamshire was forced into the greenwood. His name was Robin Hood, and he was the finest archer England had ever seen.

The story of his outlawry varies. Some say he killed one of the king's deer to feed his starving family and was declared an outlaw. Others say he slew a band of the king's foresters who mocked his archery and then tried to arrest him for poaching. In the oldest ballad, Robin Hood's Progress to Nottingham, he was but fifteen years old when a wager with fifteen foresters turned to blood, and he fled to the forest with only his bow and his wits.

In Sherwood Forest, Robin found others like himself: men who had been wronged by the unjust laws of Prince John and the corrupt Sheriff of Nottingham. There was Little John, the giant who became Robin's second-in-command after a legendary quarterstaff duel on a narrow bridge. There was Much the Miller's Son, Will Scarlet the fiery one, and Friar Tuck, the jolly monk who could fight as well as he could pray. They lived in the greenwood, sleeping beneath the Major Oak, feasting on venison stolen from the king's deer, and wearing clothes of Lincoln green that blended with the leaves.

Robin's code was simple: he robbed from the rich, especially from corrupt churchmen and nobles who had grown fat on the suffering of the poor, and he gave to those in need. Widows were protected. Poor farmers were fed. Young men unjustly sentenced were rescued from the gallows. He was a thorn in the side of the Sheriff of Nottingham, who tried time and again to capture him. The sheriff once disguised himself and joined the outlaws, but Robin saw through the ruse and escaped. He sent the sheriff home naked on a horse.

Robin's fame spread across the land. He could split a willow wand at a hundred paces. He could shoot an arrow so swiftly that it would pin a man's sleeve to a tree before the man could flinch. He was merciful to those who showed mercy and merciless to those who did not. "I love not the rich who grind the faces of the poor," he would say. "But I love the poor man who works and is honest."

When King Richard returned from the Crusades, he heard of the bold outlaw in Sherwood Forest. Disguised as a monk, the king sought out Robin and shared his meal. Impressed by Robin's honor and generosity, Richard pardoned him and his men, restoring their lands and rights. But in some versions, Robin's end was more tragic. Feeling himself growing weak, he went to Kirklees Priory to be bled by his cousin, the prioress, who betrayed him and let him bleed to death. In his final moments, he raised his horn and blew a last blast through the greenwood. Little John came running, but it was too late. Robin Hood, the prince of outlaws, had shot his last arrow.

His grave lies in Kirklees, beneath a mossy stone. But his spirit lives wherever the poor are oppressed and the rich grow too powerful. He is the eternal rebel, the archer who never misses, the man who stole from the rich and gave to the poor.
moral: Justice may require breaking unjust laws.

title: Rapunzel
summary: Once upon a time, a married couple lived next to an old witch's garden.. The wife became pregnant and craved the rapunzel plants growing there.
full_text:
Once upon a time, a married couple lived next to an old witch's garden. The wife became pregnant and craved the rapunzel plants growing there. Her husband, unable to resist her pleas, stole some plants from the garden at night.

When the witch discovered the theft, she came to their home in great anger. You have stolen my plants,' she cried, and now you must give me your child!' The couple had no choice but to surrender their newborn daughter.

The witch took the baby and named her Rapunzel after the plants she had craved. Rapunzel grew into the most beautiful girl with hair as golden as the sun. The witch kept her locked in a tall tower hidden deep in the forest, with no door and no stairs—only a window at the very top.

When Rapunzel was twelve years old, a young prince was hunting in the forest and heard beautiful singing. He followed the voice and discovered the tower. He called up to her, and Rapunzel let down her long golden hair so he could climb up. They met every day, and soon they fell deeply in love.

One day, the witch discovered their secret meetings. In her rage, she cut off all of Rapunzels beautiful hair and banished her to the desert wasteland. Then she waited for the prince with the stolen hair.

When the prince came to visit, he called up to the window and the witch let down the hair. The prince climbed up and found only the wicked witch, who laughed at him. 'You will never see your beloved Rapunzel again! she screeched.

The prince was so heartbroken that he jumped from the tower. He did not die, but thorns on the ground poked his eyes and blinded him. Wandering in despair, he wandered for years through the wilderness, eating berries and roots to survive.

One day, by some miracle, he heard a familiar voice—a woman's voice. It was Rapunzel calling out to her twins, a boy and a girl she had given birth to in the desert. The prince ran toward the voice, and when he reached her, he wept with joy. His tears fell upon her face and into his eyes, and suddenly he could see again—he had not truly been blind.

The prince took Rapunzel and the twins to his kingdom, where they lived happily ever after. The couple who had raised him took care of the children as if they were their own grandchildren.

And so the power of true love overcame all obstacles, as it always does.

title: Ra and the Secret Name
summary: Ra, the sun god, was the most powerful being in the Egyptian pantheon.. He created the world from the primordial waters of Nun.
full_text:
Ra, the sun god, was the most powerful being in the Egyptian pantheon. He created the world from the primordial waters of Nun. He sailed across the sky each day in his solar bark, the Mandjet, bringing light and warmth to the living. He descended to the underworld each night in the Mesektet, battling the serpent Apophis who sought to devour creation. Every morning, he emerged victorious, reborn, eternal.

But Ra had a secret—his true name, hidden from all gods and mortals. In Egyptian belief, to know a being's true name was to have power over them. Names were not mere labels; they were the essence of a thing. Ra's true name was the source of his invincibility. If anyone learned it, they could command him.

The goddess Isis wanted that power.

Isis was the mistress of magic, the most resourceful and clever of all the gods. She had seen her husband Osiris murdered and dismembered. She had reassembled him and raised him from the dead. She had conceived a son in the marshy swamps while hiding from Set. She understood that knowledge was survival, and survival meant knowing the secrets of the powerful.

She watched Ra, learning his habits. She noticed that as he aged—for even the sun god grew old with time—he sometimes drooled, and his saliva fell to the earth. Isis collected some of Ra's divine spittle and mixed it with the clay of the Nile. She shaped the mixture into a venomous snake and placed it at a crossroads where Ra passed each day during his journeys.

When the sun god walked by, the snake struck. Its fangs sank deep into Ra's divine flesh. The venom—made from his own essence—burned through his veins like liquid fire. Ra cried out, but the desert was empty. He stumbled home, his light flickering.

Ra fell ill. The poison spread through his body, and he began to lose control. The sun faltered in the sky. Days grew shorter. Shadows lengthened. The world trembled.

Ra called upon all the gods to help him, but none could cure what they did not understand. The fever consumed him. He grew weak, trembling, close to death.

Isis came to him then, her face full of compassion. "Father Ra," she said softly, "what ails you? Have you been bitten by something?"

"A snake," Ra whispered, his voice thin as paper. "The poison burns. I am dying."

"I can heal you," Isis said. "I am the mistress of magic. But there is a price. Tell me your secret name, Father Ra. Only by knowing it can I weave the spell that will save you. Otherwise, you will die."

Ra hesitated. He had never spoken his true name. Not to any god. Not to any mortal. If he did, he would give away his power over all creation.

The pain flared. He screamed. The sun flickered like a dying candle.

Desperate, Ra whispered his secret name—the name that held all his power, the name that had never been spoken aloud since the beginning of time. Isis leaned close. She listened. The name entered her heart like a key turning in a lock.

She chanted the words of power, weaving them with her magic, binding the poison and drawing it from Ra's body. The venom left him like smoke rising from a dying fire. Ra's eyes cleared. His strength returned. The sun blazed bright again.

But something had changed. Isis now possessed the knowledge of Ra's true name. She had power over the sun god himself. She could command him. She could unmake him.

Yet she did not abuse it.

Isis was wise. She understood that absolute power corrupts absolutely. She used the knowledge sparingly, as she had always used her magic—to protect the living, to honor the dead, to keep the balance of ma'at. She never spoke Ra's name aloud again. She never tried to command him. She simply kept the secret, a silent thread binding them.

Ra continued to sail across the sky each day. The world turned. The seasons cycled. Isis kept his secret, and the balance of the universe held.

Knowledge is power, but wisdom is knowing how to wield that power. Isis could have destroyed Ra and claimed the sky for herself. Instead, she chose cooperation, balance, the eternal order of things. Even the gods, the story reminds us, must sometimes trust each other—and keep each other's secrets. The most powerful knowledge is the kind you possess but never use.

title: Rakshasa: The Shape-Shifting Demon
summary: Ancient demons from Hindu mythology who can change form, drink blood, and cause chaos—until smarter beings outwit them.
full_text:
In the ancient, gold-dusted landscapes of India, where the laws of dharma and karma govern the cycle of existence, there exist beings of terrifying power and shifting form: the Rakshasas. These are the demons of the void, shape-shifters who dwell in the shadows of the world, feeding on the desires and fears of mortals.

The Rakshasas are not merely monsters; they are masters of illusion. A Rakshasa can appear as a beautiful maiden to lure a traveler into a trap, or as a trusted friend to sow discord among allies. They possess the ability to become invisible, to fly through the air, and to read the most secret thoughts of the human heart. Their power is rooted in trickery, and their goal is often the disruption of cosmic order.

The most legendary of all Rakshasas was Ravana, the ten-headed king of Lanka. Ravana was a scholar of the Vedas and a devotee of Shiva, but his power had corrupted his soul. He believed himself superior to the gods, and in his arrogance, he kidnapped Sita, the wife of Prince Rama. This act of defiance ignited the Great War—the Ramayana—a struggle that spanned continents and realms.

The war between Rama and Ravana was not just a battle of armies, but a clash of philosophies. Ravana represented the pinnacle of material power and intellectual brilliance divorced from morality. Rama represented the unwavering path of truth and righteousness. In the end, Ravana's illusions were shattered by Rama's arrow of truth. The fall of the ten-headed king was a victory for the light, proving that no matter how vast the power of the demon, it cannot withstand the purity of a righteous heart.

In modern India, the term "rakshasa" is still used to describe those who act with cruelty or greed. But in the deeper sense, the Rakshasa is a mirror held up to the human soul. We all have a Rakshasa within us—the part of us that is envious, arrogant, and prone to deception. The stories of the Rakshasas teach us that the greatest battle is not fought on a field of war, but within the chambers of our own mind. To defeat the Rakshasa is to conquer one's own ego and to align one's life with the eternal truth of the universe.
moral: Truth defeats illusion—but both sides believe themselves right.

title: Ra and the Snake
summary: In the golden age of the world, when the gods walked among men and the boundaries between heaven and earth were thin, Ra the sun god ruled over all creation.
full_text:
In the golden age of the world, when the gods walked among men and the boundaries between heaven and earth were thin, Ra the sun god ruled over all creation. He was the father of gods and men, the source of light, the giver of life. Every day he sailed across the sky in his solar barque, bringing warmth and radiance to the world below. Every night he journeyed through the underworld, battling the serpent Apophis to ensure the sun would rise again. He was the most powerful being in existence, but even Ra had a weakness—a secret that he guarded more carefully than any treasure.

Ra had many names, scores of them, known to gods and men alike. But he had one name that was known to no one, a secret name that he had kept hidden since the beginning of time. This name was the source of his power. As long as it remained unknown, no god, no demon, no mortal could ever challenge his authority. He kept it locked within his heart, never speaking it aloud, never writing it down, guarding it with the vigilance of a being who knew that to reveal it was to surrender everything.

But there was a goddess who coveted that name. Isis, the great enchantress, the mistress of magic, the most cunning and ambitious of all the gods, grew weary of her place in the cosmic hierarchy. She was powerful, yes, but she wanted more. She wanted to rule alongside Ra, to share in his dominion over the heavens and the earth. She knew that the secret of his power lay in his hidden name, and she resolved to discover it by any means necessary.

Daily, Ra walked the earth in the form of an aged man, his limbs growing stiff with the weight of millennia, his mouth slack, his spittle dripping to the ground. Isis followed him, watching, waiting. She gathered the earth where his saliva had fallen and mixed it with her own magic, kneading it into clay. From this clay she shaped a serpent, long and deadly, its scales gleaming with a dark luster. She breathed life into it with a spell of such power that even the underworld trembled. Then she placed the serpent on the path that Ra walked every day, hidden in the shadows where no god could see it.

The next day, Ra set out on his daily journey, surrounded by his retinue of lesser gods. He walked the familiar path, his mind on the affairs of the cosmos, unaware of the trap that awaited him. The serpent struck with the speed of lightning, sinking its fangs deep into Ra's flesh. The sun god cried out in agony, a scream that shook the heavens and made the earth tremble. The poison spread through his body like fire through dry grass, and for the first time in eternity, Ra knew fear.

His companions gathered around him in alarm. "What has happened?" they cried. "Who has done this?" But Ra could not answer. His teeth chattered, his limbs shook, the poison coursing through his veins like the floodwaters of the Nile. When at last he found his voice, he spoke of the burning pain that consumed him. "I have been wounded by something I did not see," he said. "Something that was not made by any hand I know. The fire of it spreads through my body, and I have never felt such agony."

Isis came forward, her face a mask of concern, her heart cold with purpose. "O Father Ra," she said, her voice sweet as honey, "tell me what ails you, and I shall cure you with my magic. But I must know the nature of the poison to craft the remedy. You must tell me your true name, for only then can my spell work." Ra looked at her with eyes that saw through her deception, even as the poison burned in his veins. He named one of his many names, a name of power but not the secret name. Isis shook her head. The poison did not recede. He named another, and another. Still the poison burned. "These are not enough," Isis said. "I need your true name, the name your father gave you at the dawn of creation."

The pain became unbearable. Ra felt his power waning, felt the darkness closing in. Desperate, he surrendered. In a whisper so faint that only Isis could hear, he spoke the name he had never spoken before. The secret name that was the key to his very essence. As the word left his lips, Isis felt the power of the universe flow into her. She was no longer merely a goddess. She was now equal to Ra herself.

True to her word, Isis spoke the spell of healing, and the poison fled from Ra's body. The sun god was restored, but he knew that something had changed forever. The power he had guarded for all eternity was now shared. Isis had won. From that day forward, she took her place among the greatest of the gods, the mistress of magic who had compelled even the sun to reveal its secrets. And Ra, wounded not just in body but in pride, withdrew further from the world of men, the golden age of the gods beginning its slow decline into the age of mortals.

title: Ravana
summary: Ravana, whose name means "He of the Terrifying Roar," was the most powerful demon king ever to rule the three worlds.
full_text:
Ravana, whose name means "He of the Terrifying Roar," was the most powerful demon king ever to rule the three worlds. Born to the sage Vishrava and the rakshasi princess Kaikasi, he was the grandson of the great sage Pulastya, one of the mind-born sons of Brahma. Though a demon by his mother's blood, he was a Brahmin by his father's — learned, scholarly, and deeply devout.

From childhood, Ravana exhibited extraordinary abilities. He mastered the Vedas, the sacred scriptures, and all the arts of warfare. But his greatest ambition was power. Along with his brothers Kumbhakarna and Vibhishana, Ravana performed tapasya on Mount Gokarna for ten thousand years. He offered his own heads one by one to Lord Brahma, who, pleased by such devotion, restored them and granted him a terrible boon: invulnerability against all divine beings. Only humans, whom Ravana considered beneath his notice, could harm him.

Armed with this gift, Ravana conquered the three worlds. He expelled his half-brother Kubera, the god of wealth, from Lanka — a golden city built by the celestial architect Vishwakarma — and made it his capital. He defeated the gods themselves and made them serve in his household: Agni as his cook, Varuna as his water-bearer, Vayu as his sweeper.

But Ravana's pride was his undoing. When his sister Shurpanakha was insulted by Rama and his brother Lakshmana, Ravana swore vengeance. With the help of the demon Maricha, who transformed into a golden deer, Ravana lured Rama and Lakshmana away from their hermitage. Then, disguised as a wandering ascetic, he approached Rama's wife Sita and abducted her, carrying her away in his flying chariot.

For months, Ravana tried to win Sita's affection, but she remained devoted to Rama. When Rama, aided by the monkey king Sugriva and the mighty Hanuman, crossed the ocean and laid siege to Lanka, Ravana refused to surrender. The war that followed was terrible. Finally, Rama and Ravana met in single combat. Rama shot the divine Brahmastra straight into Ravana's heart. The ten-headed king fell, and the gods celebrated the triumph of dharma over adharma. To this day, his effigy is burned every year during Dussehra, symbolizing the victory of good over evil.

title: Rich Peter the Pedlar
summary: Peter, a humble pedlar, discovers a way to acquire wealth through honest trade and a touch of serendipity. The story follows his ascent from poverty to riches and the lessons he learns about the true value of money and friendship.
full_text: Peter was a pedlar who traveled from farm to farm with a pack full of ribbons, needles, and trinkets. He was poor in coin but rich in spirit, always sharing stories with those he met. One day, he traded a simple brass button for a strange, tarnished mirror from an old hermit. The mirror did not show the face of the viewer, but rather showed the most needed object in the nearest village. Using the mirror, Peter began to provide people with exactly what they lacked—be it a missing tool, a lost heirloom, or a rare medicine. His reputation grew, and soon people traveled from leagues away to trade with him. Peter became the richest man in the region, known as 'Rich Peter.' However, he noticed that as his wealth grew, his old friends grew distant and strangers became fawning. Peter eventually gave away much of his gold to build schools and hospitals, realizing that the joy of the trade was in the helping, not the hoarding.
moral: Wealth is most valuable when it is used to benefit others rather than oneself.

title: Restoration of the Sun and Moon
summary: Through a combination of trickery, persistence, and magical songs, Wainamoinen and his companions manage to deceive Louhi into releasing the sun and moon, returning light and life to the world.
full_text: This legendary tale explores the depths of Finnish folklore. Through a combination of trickery, persistence, and magical songs, Wainamoinen and his companions manage to deceive Louhi into releasing the sun and moon, returning light and life to the world. The narrative emphasizes the magical nature of the world and the eternal struggle between order and chaos, reflecting the spiritual beliefs of the ancient North.
moral: The balance of nature must be respected.

title: Raven and the First Woman
summary: In this creation myth, the trickster god Raven wanders the shores of the world and finds a beautiful woman made of clay or shell. With a touch of his magic, he brings her to life, creating the first human woman. This story establishes Raven as the architect of human life and the source of the spark of consciousness in the Arctic world.
full_text: After Raven had stolen the light and placed it in the sky, the world was bright but empty. Raven grew lonely. One day, as he picked along the shoreline, he found a piece of driftwood carved by the waves into the shape of a woman. Her features were smooth, her hair was woven from seaweed, and her eyes were two small shells that glittered. Raven was entranced. He breathed into her mouth, and the shells became real eyes. He touched her chest, and a heart began to beat. He brushed his beak across her lips, and color flooded into her cheeks. The woman sat up and smiled. 'Who are you?' she asked. 'I am Raven,' he said. 'I am the maker of light and the creator of life.' But the woman did not bow. She stood and walked along the shore, marveling at the world. She named the plants and the animals, and her voice gave them their spirits. Raven watched with a mixture of pride and jealousy. 'I have created something that does not need me,' he realized. From this first woman, all the people of the Arctic were born.
moral: True creation is letting go; the greatest gift is independence.

title: Rana Niejta: Daughter of the Green Earth
summary: Rana Niejta is the Sámi spring goddess, daughter of Radien-attje and Raedieahkka. Her name means 'the green' or 'the fertile fields.' She makes the southern slopes of mountains green so hungry reindeer have food after winter. She is one of three divine sisters: Sala Niejta (Sun's daughter) ends winter's cold, Rana Niejta brings forth vegetation, and Saivo Niejta governs the underworld. Together they control the seasonal cycle.
full_text: Rana Niejta is the daughter of Radien-attje, the high god, and Raedieahkka, the mother goddess. Her name means 'the green one,' for she is the color of spring grass and new leaves. When the long winter finally loosens its grip on Sápmi, Rana Niejta walks across the land. Where her feet touch, the snow melts. Where her hands brush, the first flowers push through the soil. She makes the southern slopes of mountains green so that the hungry reindeer, thin from winter, can find food. She is one of three sisters who together control the wheel of the year. Sala Niejta, the Sun's daughter, ends winter's cold by bringing back the light. Rana Niejta follows with vegetation and fertility. Saivo Niejta governs the underworld, receiving the life that fades in autumn. Together they turn the seasons, sister after sister, light after green after darkness. A bronze statue of Rana Niejta stands in Mo i Rana, Norway, her arms outstretched as if blessing the land. Some say the name of Rana municipality itself comes from her name, a tribute to the goddess who makes the north green.
moral: The turning of the seasons is guided by divine hands, each sister playing her part.

title: Rávga: The Undead Sea Creature
summary: Rávga is an undead sea creature in Sámi folklore, the soul of a drowned person whose body was never found and properly buried. It appears as a long-haired, large-mouthed creature in human form. It harasses sailors and people on shore by calling out and imitating the speech of the living. It can be driven away by reading the funeral service to it.
full_text: When a fisherman drowns in the Arctic waters and his body is never recovered, his soul becomes a rávga. It rises from the sea with long hair plastered across its face and a mouth stretched wide in eternal longing. The rávga does not attack with violence. It harasses. It calls out to sailors in the voices of their loved ones, imitating speech so perfectly that even the most experienced fisherman might be fooled. 'Come closer,' the rávga calls. 'I am cold. I am lost. Take me home.' Those who follow the voice never return. The only way to drive a rávga away is to read the funeral service over the water. The words of the burial rite, spoken aloud, allow the restless soul to finally accept its death and sink back into the deep. The rávga is not evil, the Sámi say. It is only sad, caught between the world of the living and the peace of the dead, waiting for a funeral it never received.
moral: Even the dead need closure; without proper rites, the soul cannot rest.

title: Risin og Kellingin (The Giant and the Witch)
summary: Two giant sea stacks off the northern coast of Eysturoy, near Eiði. Legend says that giants in Iceland grew envious of the Faroe Islands and sent a giant and his wife (a witch) to drag the islands back to Iceland. The giant stood in the sea while the witch climbed the mountain Eiðiskollur with a heavy rope to tie the islands together. They struggled all night but could not move the islands. At dawn, sunlight struck them and turned them both to stone. The 71m stack (Risin/the Giant) and 68m stack (Kellingin/the Witch) remain visible from Tjørnuvík on Streymoy, staring longingly west toward Iceland.
full_text: Two giant sea stacks off the northern coast of Eysturoy, near Eiði. Legend says that giants in Iceland grew envious of the Faroe Islands and sent a giant and his wife (a witch) to drag the islands back to Iceland. The giant stood in the sea while the witch climbed the mountain Eiðiskollur with a heavy rope to tie the islands together. They struggled all night but could not move the islands. At dawn, sunlight struck them and turned them both to stone. The 71m stack (Risin/the Giant) and 68m stack (Kellingin/the Witch) remain visible from Tjørnuvík on Streymoy, staring longingly west toward Iceland.
moral: Pride and greed are punished by nature itself.
source: Wikipedia - Risin og Kellingin; Framtak.com; The Adventure Seekers

title: Rinkusteinar (The Rocking Stones of Oyndarfjørður)
summary: Two large boulders rest at the shore near Oyndarfjørður, constantly rocking back and forth with the rhythm of the waves. Legend says that a local sorceress (witch) saw pirate ships approaching her village after they had already attacked Fuglafjørður, robbing and killing. She rushed to the shore and cast a spell, transforming the pirate ships into stone. The two rocking stones are the petrified vessels, eternally rocking in the waves but never able to reach the village. Though often called Viking ships in modern sources, the raiders were actually Irish pirates. The natural phenomenon of their motion continues to baffle visitors.
full_text: Two large boulders rest at the shore near Oyndarfjørður, constantly rocking back and forth with the rhythm of the waves. Legend says that a local sorceress (witch) saw pirate ships approaching her village after they had already attacked Fuglafjørður, robbing and killing. She rushed to the shore and cast a spell, transforming the pirate ships into stone. The two rocking stones are the petrified vessels, eternally rocking in the waves but never able to reach the village. Though often called Viking ships in modern sources, the raiders were actually Irish pirates. The natural phenomenon of their motion continues to baffle visitors.
moral: Even in stone, a story can endure.
source: Visit Runavík; Superstition Sam - Witches and Wizards of the Faroe Islands

title: Rainbow Crow (Mànàka'has)
summary: In the time before fire, the world was cold and dark. The animals suffered, unable to keep warm or cook their food. Kishelamakank, the Creator, took pity on them and placed a great fire at the top of a high mountain, but only the birds could reach it. The crow, who in those days had a beautiful rainbow-colored song and iridescent feathers, volunteered to fetch the fire. Crow flew to the summit and seized a burning coal in his beak. The flames scorched his colorful feathers, turning them black, and the smoke choked his beautiful voice into a harsh caw. Despite the pain, crow carried the fire down to the people. The Creator honored crow's sacrifice by declaring that his black feathers would forever shimmer with the rainbow's ghost, visible when the sun catches them at the right angle — a reminder of his noble deed.
source: native-languages.org Lenape Legends; Delaware oral tradition

title: Raven Mocker (Kâ'lanû Ahkyeli'skï) (Cherokee)
summary: Kâ'lanû Ahkyeli'skï, the Raven Mocker, is a malevolent Cherokee spirit that preys on the dying. These witches are said to be ordinary-looking people during the day who transform at night into raven-like spirits. They gather around the home of a dying person and torment them, stealing their remaining years of life for themselves. The Raven Mocker flies with a sound like rushing wind and can only be seen by medicine men. After a person dies, the Raven Mockers eat the heart of the victim. The only defense against a Raven Mocker is a medicine man who knows the proper rituals to drive them away. Raven Mockers are visible only to medicine men, who can identify them even in their human form by their sleepless, bloodshot eyes and their habit of appearing exhausted from their nighttime flights.

title: Raven Steals the Sun, Moon, and Stars (Haida)
summary: In the time before light, Raven (Nang Kilslas) was tired of living in darkness. He discovered an old chief who kept all the light of the universe hidden in a nested set of five boxes. Raven transformed himself into a hemlock needle and dropped into the river where the chief's daughter came to drink. She swallowed the needle and became pregnant, giving birth to Raven-child. The old chief adored his grandchild. Raven cried incessantly until the chief allowed him to open the boxes one by one. First the stars escaped, then the moon, then the northern lights. Finally Raven grabbed the sun in his beak and flew through the smoke hole, releasing light into the world forever. This core Haida myth explains the origin of all celestial light and establishes Raven as both a trickster and culture hero who brought essential gifts to humanity despite his selfish motives.
source: Haida oral tradition; John Reed Swanton, Haida Texts and Myths; The Earth Stories Collection

title: Raven and the Moon Woman (Haida)
summary: Raven became the son of Moon Woman and cried incessantly, saying 'Boo-hoo, moon!' His mother, exhausted by his noise, stopped up all the holes in the house and untied the strings of a box containing the moon, letting Raven play with it. When she left, Raven turned into a raven and flew about the house with the moon in his beak. He then cried for the smoke hole to be opened, and when his mother obliged, he flew through it with the moon under his wing. He perched on a house top and called like a raven. Raven flew to the Nass River where fishermen were taking olachen in total darkness. He bargained with them, offering light in exchange for their spruce needles. When they mocked him, he let them see a sliver of the moon, and they gave him many olachen. He then flew to a high mountain, broke the moon in half, threw one half into the sky for the night and the other for the day, and scattered fragments as stars.
source: Haida oral tradition; Wikisource Myths and Legends of British North America

title: Raven Steals the Light (Tlingit - Yeil)
summary: In the Tlingit version of this foundational myth, Yeil (Raven) was born as the son of Kit-ka'ositiyi-qâ and was instructed and trained in every way to make a world. At that time there was no light in the world, but Raven knew that far up the Nass River was a large house where a chief kept light just for himself. Raven turned himself into a pine needle and dropped into the drinking cup of the chief's daughter. She swallowed it and gave birth to Raven as a human baby. The grandfather loved the child and eventually gave him the bundles containing the stars. Raven scattered them across the sky. He cried for the moon and pushed it through the smoke hole. Finally he cried for the box of daylight, turned into his true raven form, and flew out with the daylight under his wing. The Tlingit version emphasizes Raven's dual nature as creator and trickster, and explains not only the origin of light but also the creation of human beings from leaves after the great flood.
source: John R. Swanton, Tlingit Myths and Texts (1909); Tlingit oral tradition

title: Raven and the Fog Woman (Tlingit)
summary: Raven wanted to marry and went to the chief called Fog-Over-The-Salmon, who had a young daughter. The chief agreed but made Raven promise to treat her with respect. They married and lived contentedly through summer and fall, but winter brought famine. Raven's wife began making a basket, and despite Raven's testy comments, she completed it. The next morning she washed her hands in the basket and salmon appeared, the first salmon ever created. Every day she did this and their house was soon full of drying salmon. But Raven forgot his good fortune and quarreled with his wife daily. In a rage, he hit her with a piece of dried salmon. She fled toward the water, and when Raven tried to catch her, his hands passed through her body as if through mist. All the salmon followed her into the sea. She disappeared into the fog, for she was Fog Woman herself. Raven went to his father-in-law to beg for her return, but Chief Fog-Over-The-Salmon refused, saying Raven had broken his promise.
source: John R. Swanton, Tlingit Myths and Texts (1909); Tlingit oral tradition adapted by University of Alaska Fairbanks

title: Raven Pulls the Salmon (Tlingit)
summary: After Raven brought light to the world and created rivers and lakes, there were still no salmon in the coastal waters. The salmon, herrings, and ooligans were kept in a magical house in the middle of the Pacific Ocean, where they spawned without ever coming to shore. Only rich chiefs could paddle out to catch them, leaving the poor hungry. Raven heard of Xanaax.atwaayaa, a mythical being who possessed an octopus-tentacle staff with supernatural powers. Raven tricked the being into trading the staff by demonstrating a magical bow and arrow (actually a magpie) that seemed to kill a mountain goat from impossible distances. With the staff, Raven went to the entrance of the Alsek River and latched it to the salmon house. He struggled mightily, and his nephew Crow urged him to sing a song about Xanaax.atwaayaa. When Raven sang, he easily pulled all the salmon into the bays and rivers. The tracks of Raven's struggle can still be seen at the Alsek River entrance.
source: Tlingit legend as told by Henry Davis, Sheldon Jackson College; University of Alaska Fairbanks curriculum

title: Raven and the Beaver (Tlingit)
summary: Near a bay near Kôts!ê'L! there was a seawater pond where a beaver lived. Raven very much wanted to catch and kill this beaver, so he dug two trenches to drain the lake at low tide. After the water ran out and the beaver was visible at the bottom, Raven lowered a hook and pulled the beaver up. After killing the beaver, Raven also killed a big whale and got his people to tow it to the place where the beaver had lived. He commanded four large canoes full of people to tow the whale up the rapids through one of the canals he had made. After they labored for many days and grew tired, Raven told them to take it easy. Finally, even Raven grew tired and said, 'Turn into stone.' All did so, and to this day you can see a large island shaped like a whale with a string of four smaller islands extending from it. This Tlingit etiological myth explains a distinctive geological formation near Sitka.
source: John R. Swanton, Tlingit Myths and Texts (1909)

title: Raven Turns Himself into a Woman (Tlingit)
summary: Having lost his wife Fog Woman, Raven decided to marry again so people would look after him. Rather than finding a good wife, he turned himself into a woman and married a man from the Killerwhale people. As a wife, Raven was lazy and deceitful — she stole from the Killerwhale people. Eventually she decided to kill her husband and all the Killerwhales in the village. After this destruction, the story continues with Raven visiting Fish Hawk, promising to be a good partner and share work and food. But Raven never did any work; he ate and ate while Fish Hawk provided everything. Whenever Fish Hawk grew angry, Raven flattered him, praising his wealth and generosity. Finally Fish Hawk refused to feed Raven any longer and left him to find his own food. This comic story showcases Raven's laziness, deceit, and clever manipulation, using flattery to exploit others, while also serving as a moral lesson about the consequences of trusting flatterers.
source: John R. Swanton, Tlingit Myths and Texts (1909); adapted by University of Alaska Fairbanks

title: Raven Steals the Sun (Nuu-chah-nulth)
summary: In the Nuu-chah-nulth version of this foundational myth, there was a time before sunlight when light was kept securely in a box guarded by a great chief sometimes called the Old Fisherman. Raven wanted this light and attempted various methods to release it. He transformed himself into a floating leaf that ended up in the teacup of the great chief's daughter. She accidentally ate the leaf and in due time gave birth to Son of Raven, who was actually Raven reborn. The child was doted upon by the chief, his grandfather. Son of Raven begged to see the box of light, and after persistent pleading, the chief relented and opened the box, releasing the light. Son of Raven immediately transformed back into Raven, seized the light, and flew high into the sky. He placed the light in the sky, creating the sun that shines down on all. This myth establishes Raven as the Nuu-chah-nulth's primary culture hero and explains the origin of day and night.
source: Nuu-chah-nulth oral tradition; The Canadian Encyclopedia

title: Raven Annoys Octopus (Nuu-chah-nulth)
summary: One morning as the tide went out, the old people came to sit and watch the ocean. They saw a woman walking along the beach with her hair long and strung into eight braids — she was Octopus, carrying a digging stick to look for clams. She sat down on a rock and began to dig. Soon another person came along the beach, tall with glossy black hair — it was Raven. The old people predicted he would bother Octopus, and they knew it was not a good idea. Raven walked right down to the rock where Octopus sat and began bothering her. He teased and pestered her while she tried to dig for clams. The story serves as a humorous cautionary tale about respecting others' boundaries and not interfering with people who are working. Raven's persistent meddling backfires, teaching him (and the listener) that pestering others leads to unpleasant consequences. The Nuu-chah-nulth version emphasizes Raven's trickster nature and his tendency to interfere in others' affairs.

title: Raven Becomes Voracious (Tsimshian)
summary: This Tsimshian myth explains how Raven acquired his insatiable appetite and voracious nature. Before this story, Raven was not known for his hunger. Through a series of events involving trickery, greed, and supernatural encounters, Raven's appetite grew beyond all bounds. He began devouring everything in sight, from salmon to berries, from seal meat to olachen grease. His hunger became so legendary that it defined his character. The story serves as an etiological myth explaining why ravens and crows are scavengers who eat constantly and are always hungry. It also carries a moral lesson about the dangers of greed and insatiable desire. Raven's voraciousness is both a humorous trait — he is constantly scheming to get food — and a serious warning about what happens when consumption becomes uncontrolled. The story is part of the larger Tsimshian Raven cycle that portrays Txamsem as both a world-creating culture hero and a deeply flawed, comic figure.
source: Franz Boas, Tsimshian Mythology; Tsimshian oral tradition

title: Raven Brings Light (Nuxalk - Qwaxw)
summary: In the beginning, when the world was covered in darkness, the people were in constant sadness and prayed to the Creator Alhkw'ntam for light. Alhkw'ntam held the sun in a box in his longhouse in Nusmata (the land above) and let no one see it. The Four Carpenters who made the world were sitting around a fire when the eldest, Yulm, grabbed a piece of charcoal and shook it in his hands. When he opened his hands, it was a bird. The second Carpenter made its wings, the third made its eyes, and the youngest gave it life. They asked the bird to say its name and it flew into the sky crying 'Qwaxw, qwaxw, qwaxw!' — they named it Qwaxw, Raven. The people begged Raven to get the light. He flew to Nusmata and observed Alhkw'ntam's granddaughter Skimina. He transformed into eagle down, then a hemlock needle, but she blew him away. Finally he muddied her drinking water, and she drank him in. She became pregnant and Raven was born as her child. Raven cried for the box holding the light until Alhkw'ntam gave in. Raven broke the globe against the wall, and light leaked through the smoke vent to become the sun, stars, and moon. The people were no longer sad.
source: Nuxalk oral tradition; Nuxalk Nation official website; Franz Boas, The Mythology of the Bella Coola Indians

title: Robin and Blue-Jay (Chinook)
summary: Blue Jay and Robin, two brothers, were hungry. Blue Jay called a large fish (the sleeper) to come ashore through trickery and speared it. When they returned home, Robin asked for the tail, breast, and head, making Blue Jay angry. He said that the Q!têê'nse (a supernatural monster) would eat the fish instead. Robin cried and left. Blue Jay softened and called his brother back, giving him all the choice parts. Later, Blue Jay dreamed that people were sending for him to cure a sick person. Blue Jay and Robin journeyed to cure a duck who was choking, receiving payment of a lake's worth of roots. Next they cured a wolf girl who was choking on an elk's kneecap, receiving two canoes full of meat and grease. But Blue Jay's arrogance and greed in demanding excessive payment led to consequences. The story demonstrates the Chinook trickster cycle's typical structure: Blue Jay is boastful and selfish but ultimately provides for his family through his supernatural curing abilities, while long-suffering Robin represents patience and humility.
source: Franz Boas, Chinook Texts (1894); Chinook oral tradition from Charles Cultee

title: Ruwa and the Sacred Mountain Kilimanjaro
summary: Ruwa, the supreme god of the Chaga people, dwells upon the snow-capped peak of Kilimanjaro, the highest mountain in Africa. According to Chaga cosmology, Ruwa is associated with the sun and resides in the sky alongside his consort, the moon. From his celestial throne on Kibo peak, Ruwa watches over the Chaga people and all creation. The mountain is not merely a mountain but the physical manifestation of Ruwa's presence on earth — its glaciers are his crown, its forests his garments, its rivers his blessings flowing down to nourish the people. Sacrifices to Ruwa are performed at high noon, when the sun is at its zenith, in sacred groves on the mountain's slopes. The spirits of Chaga chiefs (Mangi) who have passed away join Ruwa on the mountain, interceding for their descendants. The Chaga believe that the mountain's glaciers will never fully melt, for as long as they remain, Ruwa's protection endures. Kilimanjaro is thus the sacred center of Chaga spiritual life.
source: Chaga oral tradition, Charles Dundas 'Kilimanjaro and Its People'
moral: The divine presence manifests in the most magnificent features of the natural world.

title: Rangi and Papa: The Separation of Sky and Earth
summary: In the beginning, Ranginui (Sky Father) and Papatuanuku (Earth Mother) lay locked in a tight embrace, leaving their children trapped in darkness between them. The children grew weary of the cramped darkness and debated how to gain freedom. Tane Mahuta, god of forests, proposed separating their parents. While Tawhirimatea opposed, Tane lay on his back and pushed upward with his feet, forcing Rangi upward and thrusting Papa down. Thus light entered the world. Tawhirimatea, enraged, joined his father in the sky and sent storms against his siblings, beginning the eternal conflict between wind, forest, sea, and land.

title: Raven the Creator (Nivkh Tradition)
summary: The Nivkh creation myth centered on the Raven, a central figure shared with neighboring Chukchi and Koryak traditions. In Nivkh mythology, the Raven (known as Kurka or similar names) created the world by diving into the primordial sea and bringing up earth on his beak. He shaped the land of Sakhalin Island and the Amur River estuary, and then created human beings from clay mixed with his own feathers. The Raven is both creator and trickster who taught the Nivkh people how to fish, build canoes, and conduct the sacred bear ceremony. The myths also explain how the Raven brought the sun and moon into the sky by stealing them from a powerful spirit. Nivkh folklore was first systematically recorded by Bronisław Piłsudski and later by Vladimir Sangi.

title: Roro Jonggrang and the Legend of Prambanan Temple
summary: A Javanese legend explaining the origin of Prambanan Temple. Bandung Bondowoso, a powerful prince with supernatural abilities and an army of invisible genies, fell in love with Princess Roro Jonggrang. When she rejected his proposal, he attacked her father's kingdom and conquered it. Forced to consider marriage, Roro Jonggrang set an impossible condition: Bandung must build 1,000 temples in one night. Using his supernatural army, he nearly completed the task. Desperate, Roro Jonggrang ordered her maids to light fires and pound rice, tricking the spirits into thinking dawn had arrived. Enraged by the deception, Bandung cursed Roro Jonggrang, transforming her into the 1,000th statue — which remains at Prambanan to this day.
notes: Explains the origin of Prambanan Temple, a UNESCO World Heritage site in Central Java.
moral: True honor lives on in stories passed down through generations.

title: Rannamaari: The Sea Demon That Demanded Sacrifice
summary: In pre-Islamic Maldives, a terrible sea demon called Rannamaari rose from the ocean each month to demand a sacrificed virgin. The girls were dressed in fine clothes and left alone in a Buddhist temple on the eastern seafront of Malé; by dawn, they were found violated and dead. This terror continued until a Maghrebi Muslim traveler, Abu'l-Barakat al-Barbari (or Yusuf al-Tabrezi from Tabriz, Iran), volunteered to take the girl's place. He spent the night reciting the Quran, and when the demon appeared, the holy verses caused it to flee into the sea, never to return. The king, identified as Dhovemi, converted to Islam and took the name Sultan Muhammad ibn Abdullah in 1153 CE, and the Maldives became a Muslim nation. Historians note parallels with Buddhist tantric rituals, and folklorist Xavier Romero-Frias traces the name Rannamaari to a South Indian goddess.
source: Ibn Battuta travelogue (14th century), Isdhoo Loamaafaanu copper plates, Xavier Romero-Frias research
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

title: River Mumma and the Golden Table
summary: River Mumma is a female water spirit who guards the sources of Jamaica's rivers. She is sometimes seen sitting by the river, combing her long hair with a golden comb, leaving the comb on rocks to entice victims whom she then draws into the water. Associated with her is the Legend of the Golden Table: a table of pure gold lies at the bottom of certain rivers, rising briefly to the surface at midday. Whoever sees it becomes mesmerized and obsessed with obtaining it, but efforts to procure the table always end in disaster as the river claims their lives. The legend likely arose from the Spanish quest for gold in Jamaica. In slavery times, sacrifices were offered to River Mumma in drought.
source: Real Jamaica Vacations; Jamaican oral tradition
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

title: Rostam and Sohrab
summary: The most famous tragedy of the Persian Shahnameh. Rostam, Iran's greatest hero, travels to Samangan where he marries Princess Tahmineh. He returns to Iran, leaving her pregnant with Sohrab. Years later, Sohrab, now Turan's mightiest warrior, leads an army against Iran, hoping to find his father. Unaware of each other's identity, father and son meet in single combat. After a grueling battle, Rostam throws Sohrab and stabs him. Only then does Sohrab show the armband amulet given by Tahmineh. Rostam discovers he has killed his own son. Despite desperate pleas for medicine, Kay Kavus withholds it. Sohrab dies in his father's arms, and Tahmineh burns all her possessions in grief.

title: Rostam's Seven Trials (Haft Khan-e Rostam)
summary: To rescue King Kay Kavus, captured by the White Demon (Div-e Sepid) in Mazandaran, Rostam undertakes seven impossible labors: (1) Killing Rakhsh's attacker, a ferocious lion; (2) Surviving a waterless desert with his horse Rakhsh; (3) Slaying a monstrous dragon; (4) Killing a powerful witch through divine discernment; (5) Capturing the warrior Olad to serve as guide; (6) Defeating the giant Arzhang Div; and (7) The ultimate trial — battling and killing the White Demon (Div-e Sepid) himself in the demon's cave, using the demon's own liver to restore the blinded king's sight. This epic is the Persian equivalent of Hercules' labors.

title: Ra and the Hidden Name
summary: Isis, the great enchantress, desired power equal to Ra himself. As Ra aged, saliva dripped from his mouth and fell upon the ground. Isis gathered the saliva, mixed it with earth, and shaped it into a venomous serpent which she placed on Ra's path. When Ra was bitten by the invisible serpent, he suffered agonizing pain. Isis approached him and offered to cure him—but only if he would reveal his secret, hidden name—the name that gave him power over all gods and creation. Ra first listed his known names: 'I am Khepera in the morning, Ra at noon, Atum in the evening. I am the maker of heaven and earth, the creator of the gods.' But Isis insisted: 'Your name is not among those you have told me.' Finally, in agony, Ra consented. He asked Isis to leave him so he could pass his name from his heart to hers, and the secret power was transferred. Isis then commanded the poison to leave Ra's body, and he was healed. The myth explains how Isis gained her great magical knowledge and power.
source: Wisdom Library - Legend of Ra and Isis; snip Sacred Texts
